---
title: "Untitled"
format: html
---

```{python}
import os
import whitebox
import rasterio
from rasterio.mask import mask
import numpy as np
import geopandas as gpd
from shapely.geometry import Point
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pysheds
from pysheds.view import Raster, ViewFinder
from pysheds.grid import Grid


wbt = whitebox.WhiteboxTools()
wbt.verbose = True
```

```{python}
# specify paths to existing files and set filenames for outputs

data_dir = "/Users/dillencorlett/pbr/data"

dem_raw      = os.path.join(data_dir, "bartleson_dem.tif")
dem_smoothed = os.path.join(data_dir, "bartleson_dem_smoothed.tif")
dem_hydro = os.path.join(data_dir, "dem_hydroconditioned_b.tif")
flowdir     = os.path.join(data_dir, "flowdir_b.tif")
flowacc     = os.path.join(data_dir, "flowacc_b.tif")
streams    = os.path.join(data_dir, "streamnetwork_b.tif")
watershed = os.path.join(data_dir, "bartleson_watershed.shp")
streamclip = os.path.join(data_dir, "streams_clipped_b.tif")

print("Raw DEM exists?", os.path.exists(dem_raw))
```

```{python}
# load dem as pysheds object

grid = Grid.from_raster(dem_raw)

pydem = grid.read_raster(dem_raw)
```

```{python}
# fix nodata value issue

pydem[pydem == -999999.0] = np.nan
```

```{python}
# smooth DEM to reduce road and artifact influence before hydrological conditioning
# feature_preserving_smoothing is designed for high-resolution LiDAR DEMs:
# it removes fine-scale noise (roads, berms) while preserving real geomorphic
# structure like ridges and channel edges.
# filter: kernel size (larger = more smoothing), norm_diff: edge-preservation
# threshold (lower = sharper edge preservation), num_iter: smoothing passes

wbt.feature_preserving_smoothing(
    dem_raw,
    dem_smoothed,
    filter=11,
    norm_diff=15,
    num_iter=3
)

# reload smoothed DEM into pysheds
grid = Grid.from_raster(dem_smoothed)
pydem = grid.read_raster(dem_smoothed)
pydem[pydem == -999999.0] = np.nan

print("Smoothed DEM exists?", os.path.exists(dem_smoothed))
```

```{python}
# visual check of DEM

dem_view = grid.view(pydem)

plt.imshow(grid.view(dem_view), cmap='terrain', zorder=1)
```

skipping these for now because it they don't work with the wbt smoothing
```{python}
# filling pits

#filled_dem = grid.fill_pits(pydem)
#pits = grid.detect_pits(filled_dem)
#assert not pits.any()
```

```{python}
#dem_view = grid.view(filled_dem)

#plt.imshow(grid.view(dem_view), cmap='terrain', zorder=1)
```

```{python}
# filling depressions

#flooded_dem = grid.fill_depressions(filled_dem)
#depressions = grid.detect_depressions(flooded_dem)
#assert not depressions.any()
```

```{python}
# resolving flats

#inflated_dem = grid.resolve_flats(flooded_dem)
#flats = grid.detect_flats(inflated_dem)
#assert not flats.any()
```

```{python}
# save hydroconditioned dem to file

#grid.to_raster(inflated_dem, dem_hydro)
```

```{python}
# flow direction

fdir = grid.flowdir(pydem)

grid.to_raster(fdir, flowdir)
```

```{python}
# flow accumulation

acc = grid.accumulation(fdir)

grid.to_raster(acc, flowacc)
```

```{python}
# extract streams

wbt.extract_streams(flowacc, streams, threshold=1000)
print("streams exists?", os.path.exists(streams))
```

```{python}
# reading in watershed boundary

watershed_poly = gpd.read_file(watershed)
```

```{python}
# clip stream raster to watershed boundary

with rasterio.open(streams) as src:
    watershed_poly = watershed_poly.to_crs(src.crs)
    streams_clipped, out_transform = mask(src, watershed_poly.geometry, crop=True)
    out_meta = src.meta.copy()

with rasterio.open(streamclip,'w',**out_meta) as dst:
    dst.write(streams_clipped)
```

```{python}
# sanity check

for f in [dem_hydro, flowdir, flowacc, streamclip]:
    print(f"{os.path.basename(f)} exists? {os.path.exists(f)}")
```

the code from here down runs, but doesn't generate the correct pour point. theorizing that it's because of the resolution of our DEM (too much detail), experimenting with wbt smoothing thresholds to see if that removes some of the interference from roads.

```{python}
# -------------------------------------------------------------------
# Pour Point Estimation — pysheds-native workflow
#
# The outlet is the cell on the watershed boundary with the highest
# flow accumulation — this is true by definition and requires no
# D8-exit or stream-network pre-filtering (those filters can exclude
# the true outlet when the polygon edge doesn't align perfectly with
# the raster grid).
#
# After finding that boundary-maximum cell we refine by snapping to
# the nearest high-accumulation (stream) cell via
# grid.snap_to_mask(), landing exactly on the extracted network.
# -------------------------------------------------------------------

from rasterio.features import rasterize as rio_rasterize
from rasterio.transform import xy as raster_xy
from scipy.ndimage import binary_erosion

# --- Rasterize watershed polygon onto the pysheds grid ---
# grid.affine and grid.shape match dem_raw exactly
ws_poly_b = gpd.read_file(watershed).to_crs(grid.crs)

ws_mask_arr = rio_rasterize(
    [(geom, 1) for geom in ws_poly_b.geometry],
    out_shape=grid.shape,
    transform=grid.affine,
    fill=0,
    dtype="uint8"
).astype(bool)

# --- Boundary pixels: inside watershed, adjacent to at least one outside cell ---
ws_boundary_arr = ws_mask_arr & ~binary_erosion(ws_mask_arr, structure=np.ones((3, 3)))

# --- Pull accumulation array from the pysheds Raster object ---
acc_view = np.array(grid.view(acc, dtype=float))
if acc.nodata is not None:
    acc_view[acc_view == acc.nodata] = np.nan

# --- Find the boundary pixel with maximum accumulation ---
acc_on_boundary = np.where(ws_boundary_arr, acc_view, np.nan)
best_r, best_c = np.unravel_index(np.nanargmax(acc_on_boundary), acc_on_boundary.shape)
rough_x, rough_y = raster_xy(grid.affine, best_r, best_c)

print(f"Boundary max-acc pixel: row={best_r}, col={best_c}")
print(f"  Coordinates: x={rough_x:.4f}, y={rough_y:.4f}")
print(f"  Flow Acc:    {acc_on_boundary[best_r, best_c]:.0f} cells")
```

```{python}
# --- Snap to the nearest stream cell ---
# stream_mask mirrors the threshold used in wbt.extract_streams above.
# snap_to_mask() walks outward from the rough point until it hits a
# True cell, correcting any small misalignment between polygon edge
# and raster grid.

stream_mask_arr = acc_view > 1000   # match wbt.extract_streams threshold
stream_mask = Raster(stream_mask_arr, viewfinder=acc.viewfinder)

pp_snapped = grid.snap_to_mask(stream_mask, np.array([[rough_x, rough_y]]))
pp_x, pp_y = float(pp_snapped[0][0]), float(pp_snapped[0][1])

# --- Elevation at snapped point ---
dem_view_arr = np.array(grid.view(inflated_dem, dtype=float))
if inflated_dem.nodata is not None:
    dem_view_arr[dem_view_arr == inflated_dem.nodata] = np.nan

# Nearest pixel indices for attribute lookup
snap_c = int(round((pp_x - grid.affine.c) / grid.affine.a))
snap_r = int(round((grid.affine.f - pp_y) / abs(grid.affine.e)))
snap_r = int(np.clip(snap_r, 0, grid.shape[0] - 1))
snap_c = int(np.clip(snap_c, 0, grid.shape[1] - 1))

print(f"\nSnapped Pour Point:")
print(f"  Coordinates: x={pp_x:.4f}, y={pp_y:.4f}")
print(f"  Elevation:   {dem_view_arr[snap_r, snap_c]:.2f}")
print(f"  Flow Acc:    {acc_view[snap_r, snap_c]:.0f} cells")

# --- Save ---
pp_gdf = gpd.GeoDataFrame(
    {"elev": [float(dem_view_arr[snap_r, snap_c])],
     "flow_acc": [float(acc_view[snap_r, snap_c])]},
    geometry=[Point(pp_x, pp_y)],
    crs=grid.crs
)
pp_path = os.path.join(data_dir, "pour_point_b.geojson")
pp_gdf.to_file(pp_path, driver="GeoJSON")
print(f"\nSaved to: {pp_path}")
```

```{python}
# --- Visualize ---

with rasterio.open(dem_raw) as src:
    dem_bounds = src.bounds

ext = [dem_bounds.left, dem_bounds.right, dem_bounds.bottom, dem_bounds.top]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 9))

# DEM masked to watershed
ax.imshow(
    np.where(ws_mask_arr, dem_view_arr, np.nan),
    cmap="terrain", extent=ext, origin="upper"
)

# Stream network overlay
ax.imshow(
    np.where(stream_mask_arr & ws_mask_arr, 1.0, np.nan),
    cmap="Blues", extent=ext, origin="upper", alpha=0.6
)

# Watershed boundary
ws_poly_b.boundary.plot(ax=ax, color="black", linewidth=1.5)

# Rough boundary-max point
ax.scatter(rough_x, rough_y, s=120, c="orange", zorder=5,
           marker="o", label="Boundary max acc")

# Snapped pour point
ax.scatter(pp_x, pp_y, s=200, c="red", zorder=6,
           marker="*", label="Pour point (snapped)")

ax.legend(fontsize=11)
ax.set_title("Estimated Pour Point — pysheds workflow")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```
